name: Machu Picchu
summary: Machu Picchu is one of Peru's safest tourist destinations with excellent security measures, low crime rates, and strong tourist police presence. While petty crime exists in surrounding areas like Cusco, the ancient citadel itself maintains strict safety protocols for its 1.5 million annual visitors.
quickVerdict: Machu Picchu stands as Peru's premier tourist destination with exceptional safety standards. The site benefits from dedicated tourist police, controlled access, and comprehensive security measures. Main concerns include altitude sickness, weather-related challenges during rainy season, and typical tourist-targeted scams in gateway cities. The US State Department specifically notes that Machu Picchu and Sacred Valley are not located in areas of concern, making it safer than many other Peruvian destinations.

neighborhoods.name: Machu Picchu Citadel
neighborhoods.description: The ancient citadel itself is extremely safe with constant surveillance, mandatory guides, controlled entry points, and immediate medical assistance available. Tourist police patrol regularly and strict regulations protect visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Aguas Calientes/Machu Picchu Pueblo
neighborhoods.description: The gateway town is tourist-focused with good security, decent lighting, and regular police presence. Generally safe for walking around, though visitors should watch belongings in crowded areas and markets.

neighborhoods.name: Sacred Valley Towns
neighborhoods.description: Ollantaytambo, Pisac, and other Sacred Valley communities are well-policed tourist areas with low crime rates. Mostly safe during day and evening hours with basic precautions.

neighborhoods.name: Cusco Historic Center
neighborhoods.description: Central Plaza de Armas area is generally safe during daylight with heavy tourist police presence, but pickpocketing increases in crowded markets and after dark. Altitude effects can impair judgment.

neighborhoods.name: Cusco San Blas Neighborhood
neighborhoods.description: Artisan quarter with narrow cobblestone streets can be poorly lit at night. Generally safe during day but requires extra caution after sunset, especially for solo travelers.

neighborhoods.name: Outer Lima Districts
neighborhoods.description: Areas like Villa El Salvador and peripheral districts have higher crime rates with limited tourist infrastructure. Visitors transiting to Machu Picchu should avoid these areas entirely.

scams.name: Fake Machu Picchu Tickets
scams.description: Unlicensed agencies and street vendors sell counterfeit tickets or overpriced tours that never materialize. Online scammers advertise impossible deals on social media platforms.
scams.howToAvoid: Only purchase tickets through official Ministry of Culture website or authorized agencies with physical Cusco offices. Verify company registration and never pay full amount upfront to street vendors.

scams.name: Overpriced Tourist Transportation
scams.description: Taxi drivers quote inflated prices or claim broken meters, especially at airports and train stations. Unlicensed drivers approach tourists with unofficial transport offers.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxi companies, rideshare apps like Uber, or pre-arranged hotel transfers. Always agree on fare before getting in vehicle and ask for receipt.

scams.name: Distraction Theft Teams
scams.description: Groups work together using spilled food, fake accidents, or asking for directions while accomplices steal bags or valuables from distracted tourists in crowded areas.
scams.howToAvoid: Keep bags secured in front of body in crowds, be suspicious of overly helpful strangers, and maintain awareness in busy tourist areas like markets and train stations.

scams.name: Photo Scams with Traditional Dress
scams.description: People in colorful traditional clothing, sometimes with baby animals, pose for photos then demand excessive payment ranging from $20-50 per picture taken.
scams.howToAvoid: Ask about costs upfront before photos, negotiate reasonable price (1-2 soles is normal), or politely decline and walk away if pressured for unreasonable amounts.

soloFemale.overview: Solo female travel to Machu Picchu is very safe and increasingly popular, with thousands of women successfully visiting annually. Tourist areas maintain higher security standards, and female-friendly infrastructure includes women-only hostel dorms and supportive traveler networks. Main concerns are street harassment in urban areas and altitude effects, but these are manageable with proper preparation.
tips: Stay in well-reviewed hostels with good security and female-only dorms when available
Connect with other travelers through hostel common areas and join group tours to Sacred Valley sites
Prepare for altitude sickness with 2-3 day acclimatization in Cusco before visiting Machu Picchu
Use official transportation like registered taxis or rideshare apps rather than street hails
Carry whistle and keep emergency contacts readily available, including tourist police numbers

nightSafety.overview: Night safety varies significantly by location. Machu Picchu itself closes at 5 PM, but Aguas Calientes has a reasonable nightlife scene for such a small town. Cusco offers vibrant nightlife in tourist zones with good police presence, though altitude effects can impair judgment. Lima requires more caution after dark.
tips: Stick to well-lit tourist areas and avoid walking alone in quieter neighborhoods after 10 PM
Use official taxis or rideshare services for transportation between venues rather than walking
Be extra cautious with alcohol consumption due to altitude effects reducing tolerance
Stay in groups when possible and let accommodation staff know your plans
Keep valuables secured and avoid displaying expensive items like jewelry or cameras

transport.metro: Peru does not have metro systems. Lima has the Metropolitan bus rapid transit system, but most Machu Picchu visitors use other transportation methods.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates in Lima and Cusco with good safety records. Cabify and local apps like Beat also available. Rideshare services offer safer, more transparent pricing than street taxis.
transport.taxis: Use only registered taxi companies or those arranged by hotels. Official airport taxis and radio-dispatched services are safest. Always agree on fare beforehand or insist on meter usage.
transport.tips: Train travel to Machu Picchu via Peru Rail or Inca Rail is safe and scenic. Bus travel between cities is generally secure with reputable companies like Cruz del Sur, though mountain roads require careful weather monitoring during rainy season.

customs: Greet people with handshake for first meetings, kiss on one cheek for friends and family members
Dress modestly in religious sites and rural communities - avoid shorts and revealing clothing
Always ask permission before photographing locals, especially those in traditional dress, and offer small tip
Say 'buen provecho' before eating and leave small amount of food on plate when finished
Respect sacred sites like Machu Picchu by staying on marked trails and not touching ruins or walls

health.overview: Altitude sickness is the primary health concern, affecting up to 30% of visitors to Cusco and surrounding areas. Machu Picchu sits at 7,970 feet while Cusco is at 11,152 feet. Healthcare in tourist areas is adequate with private clinics offering English-speaking staff.
health.water: Drink only bottled water and avoid ice cubes in drinks. Tap water is not safe for consumption. Boiled water is acceptable but takes longer at high altitude due to atmospheric pressure.
health.vaccinations: Routine vaccines should be current. Hepatitis A and typhoid recommended for all travelers. Yellow fever required only for Amazon regions, not needed for Machu Picchu. Consult travel medicine specialist 6-8 weeks before departure.
health.altitude: Acclimatize gradually by spending 2-3 days in Cusco before visiting Machu Picchu. Stay hydrated, avoid alcohol initially, consider altitude medication if recommended by doctor. Symptoms include headache, nausea, and fatigue - seek medical attention if severe.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Lima: Avenida La Encalada cdra. 17 s/n, Surco, Lima 33. Phone: +51-1-618-2000. Emergency after-hours: +51-1-618-2397.

faq.q: Is Machu Picchu safe for tourists?
faq.a: Yes, Machu Picchu is one of Peru's safest tourist destinations with excellent security measures, dedicated tourist police, and controlled access. The site maintains strict safety protocols for its 1.5 million annual visitors, though visitors should prepare for altitude effects and weather conditions.

faq.q: Is Machu Picchu safe at night?
faq.a: Machu Picchu itself closes at 5 PM, so overnight visits are prohibited. The nearby town of Aguas Calientes is generally safe for evening activities with good lighting and police presence, though visitors should use normal precautions and avoid excessive alcohol due to altitude.

faq.q: Is Machu Picchu safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, thousands of solo women visit Machu Picchu safely each year. Tourist areas have good security infrastructure, female-friendly accommodations are available, and the strong traveler community makes it easy to connect with others. Main precautions involve altitude preparation and standard urban safety awareness.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid near Machu Picchu?
faq.a: Machu Picchu itself and the Sacred Valley are very safe. Exercise more caution in outer Lima districts and avoid the VRAEM region entirely. In Cusco, be extra careful in markets and poorly lit areas after dark, but central tourist zones are generally secure.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport to reach Machu Picchu?
faq.a: Yes, the train to Machu Picchu via Peru Rail or Inca Rail is safe and the preferred method. Buses between cities with reputable companies like Cruz del Sur are also secure. Use official taxis or rideshare apps rather than street hails, and arrange airport transfers through hotels when possible.
